citations.narrative:
Areas of disrepair were observed within the enclosure housing three foxes. Multiple dig holes were present throughout the
enclosure floor including one location where the foxes had excavated the dirt substrate enough to create a hole beneath
the enclosure fencing. This hole was approximately six inches in length and the possibility of escape was imminent. The
facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. Housing
facilities must be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to safely contain
the animals. Correct by 4/3/2026.

Tree limbs, broken totes, animal carriers, miscellaneous clutter, and trash were identified between enclosures housing
foxes, raccoons, mountain and white-noised coatis, kinkajous, nutria and a skunk. The lack of housekeeping and
sanitation may attract pests into the facility, result in unpleasant odors, and may contribute to the spread of disease.
Premises (buildings and grounds) shall be kept clean and in good repair in order to protect the animals from injury and to
facilitate the prescribed husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. Accumulations of trash shall be placed in designated
areas and cleared as necessary to protect the health of the animals. Correct by 4/8/2026.

A dead mouse was found in the only water source available to the nutria. The licensee stated that this is not the first
occurrence of mice being found in the animal's water trough. The presence of pests can transmit disease, contaminate
food and water sources, and pose a health risk to the animals. A safe and effective program for the control of insects,
ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests shall be established and maintained. Correct by 4/14/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
Areas of disrepair were observed within the enclosure housing three foxes. Multiple dig holes were present throughout the 
enclosure floor including one location where the foxes had excavated the dirt substrate enough to create a hole beneath 
the enclosure fencing. This hole was approximately six inches in length and the possibility of escape was imminent. The 
facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. Housing 
facilities must be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to safely contain 
the animals. Correct by 4/3/2026. 
3.131(c) 
   
Sanitation. 
Tree limbs, broken totes, animal carriers, miscellaneous clutter, and trash were identified between enclosures housing 
foxes, raccoons, mountain and white-noised coatis, kinkajous, nutria and a skunk. The lack of housekeeping and 
sanitation may attract pests into the facility, result in unpleasant odors, and may contribute to the spread of disease. 
Premises (buildings and grounds) shall be kept clean and in good repair in order to protect the animals from injury and to 
facilitate the prescribed husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. Accumulations of trash shall be placed in designated 
areas and cleared as necessary to protect the health of the animals. Correct by 4/8/2026. 
3.131(d) 
   
Sanitation. 
A dead mouse was found in the only water source available to the nutria. The licensee stated that this is not the first 
occurrence of mice being found in the animal's water trough. The presence of pests can transmit disease, contaminate 
food and water sources, and pose a health risk to the animals. A safe and effective program for the control of insects, 
ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests shall be established and maintained. Correct by 4/14/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.

species.common: RED FOX (INCLUDES SILVER FOX & CROSS FOX)
